epigraph.text:
Two are better than one, because they have a good reward 
for their toil.

content.text:
$U$-substitution reversed the Chain Rule and unlocked an entire family of
integrals. But consider the integral
\[
 \int x\, e^{x}\dx.
\]
No substitution will untangle the product $x \cdot e^{x}$. If we set
$u = x$, then $du = dx$, and the $e^{x}$ remains with nothing to absorb.
If we set $u = e^{x}$, then $du = e^{x}\dx$, and the $x$ has nowhere to go.
We need a new tool—one that reverses not the Chain Rule, but the
**Product Rule**.

Recall the Product Rule for derivatives: if $u$ and $v$ are differentiable
functions of $x$, then
\[
 \deriv{}{x}\bigl[u(x)\,v(x)\bigr]
 = u(x)\,v'(x) + v(x)\,u'(x).
\]
We rearrange to isolate the term $u\,v'$:
\[
 u(x)\,v'(x)
 = \deriv{}{x}\bigl[u(x)\,v(x)\bigr] - v(x)\,u'(x).
\]
Now integrate both sides with respect to $x$:
\[
 \int u(x)\,v'(x)\dx
 = u(x)\,v(x) - \int v(x)\,u'(x)\dx.
\]
Writing $dv = v'(x)\dx$ and $du = u'(x)\dx$, we arrive at the compact form.

content.title: Integration by Parts
content.content:
If $u$ and $v$ are differentiable functions, then
\[
 \int u\, dv = uv - \int v\, du.
\]
For definite integrals,
\[
 \defint{a}{b} u\, dv
 = \bigl[uv\bigr]_{a}^{b} - \defint{a}{b} v\, du.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The strategy is this: given a product that resists $u$-substitution, we split
it into a part $u$ (which we differentiate) and a part $dv$ (which we
integrate). If we choose wisely, the new integral $\int v\,du$ will be
simpler than the original.

content.title: Integration by parts transforms, it doesn't always solve
content.content:
Integration by parts converts one integral into another. The goal is to make
the new integral $\int v\,du$ *easier* than the original $\int u\,dv$.
A poor choice of $u$ and $dv$ can make things worse. Always ask: "Does my
choice simplify the problem?"

content.text:
The success of integration by parts hinges on a wise choice of $u$. The
following mnemonic ranks function types in the order they should typically be
chosen for $u$:

content.title: LIATE Guideline for Choosing $u$
content.content:
When applying integration by parts, let $u$ be the first type that appears
in your integrand, reading down this list:

1. **L**ogarithmic functions: $\ln x$, $\log_{b} x$

2. **I**nverse trigonometric functions: $\arctan x$, $\arcsin x$, etc.

3. **A**lgebraic functions: $x$, $x^{2}$, $x^{n}$, polynomials

4. **T**rigonometric functions: $\sin x$, $\cos x$, $\tan x$, etc.

5. **E**xponential functions: $e^{x}$, $2^{x}$, $a^{x}$
Everything not chosen as $u$ becomes part of $dv$.

**Why does this work?** Logarithmic and inverse trig functions are hard
to integrate but easy to differentiate—so we want to differentiate them
(put them in $u$). Exponentials are trivial to integrate—so we want to
integrate them (put them in $dv$).

content.title: LIATE is a guideline, not a law
content.content:
LIATE works in the vast majority of cases, but there are exceptions. Always
check: does your choice of $u$ and $dv$ make $\int v\,du$ simpler? If not,
try a different split.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int x\,e^{x}\dx$
content.solution:
By LIATE, the algebraic factor $x$ is chosen as $u$:
\[
 u = x, \qquad dv = e^{x}\dx,
 \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
 du = dx, \qquad v = e^{x}.
\]
Applying the formula:
$$
 \int x\, e^{x}\dx
 &= x\,e^{x} - \int e^{x}\dx \\
 &= x\,e^{x} - e^{x} + C \\
 &= e^{x}(x - 1) + C.
$$
We can verify by differentiating: $\deriv{}{x}\bigl[e^{x}(x-1)\bigr]
= e^{x}(x-1) + e^{x} = x\,e^{x}$.\;

content.title: $\displaystyle\int x\sin x\dx$
content.solution:
Again, LIATE directs us to let $u = x$:
\[
 u = x, \qquad dv = \sin x\dx,
 \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
 du = dx, \qquad v = -\cos x.
\]
Then
$$
 \int x\sin x\dx
 &= -x\cos x - \int (-\cos x)\dx \\
 &= -x\cos x + \int \cos x\dx \\
 &= -x\cos x + \sin x + C.
$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int x\cos(3x)\dx$
content.solution:
Let $u = x$ and $dv = \cos(3x)\dx$. Then $du = dx$ and
$v = \frac{1}{3}\sin(3x)$. Applying integration by parts:
$$
 \int x\cos(3x)\dx
 &= x\cdot\frac{\sin(3x)}{3} - \int \frac{\sin(3x)}{3}\dx \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{x\sin(3x)}{3} + \frac{\cos(3x)}{9} + C.
$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \ln x\dx$
content.solution:
At first glance this is not a product. But we can write $\ln x = \ln x
\cdot 1$ and apply integration by parts with
\[
 u = \ln x, \qquad dv = dx,
 \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
 du = \frac{1}{x}\dx, \qquad v = x.
\]
Then
$$
 \int \ln x\dx
 &= x\ln x - \int x \cdot \frac{1}{x}\dx \\
 &= x\ln x - \int 1\dx \\
 &= x\ln x - x + C.
$$
This is a technique worth remembering: *integration by parts can handle
integrands that are not obvious products by pairing them with $dv = dx$.*

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \ln(x^{2})\dx$
content.solution:
First simplify using logarithm properties: $\ln(x^{2}) = 2\ln x$. Therefore
\[
 \int \ln(x^{2})\dx = 2\int \ln x\dx = 2(x\ln x - x) + C
 = 2x\ln x - 2x + C.
\]
Alternatively, we could write this as $x\ln(x^{2}) - 2x + C$ to match the
original form.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \arctan x\dx$
content.solution:
By LIATE, the inverse trig function takes priority:
\[
 u = \arctan x, \qquad dv = dx,
 \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
 du = \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\dx, \qquad v = x.
\]
Then
$$
 \int \arctan x\dx
 &= x\arctan x - \int \frac{x}{1+x^{2}}\dx.
$$
The remaining integral yields to the substitution $w = 1 + x^{2}$,
$dw = 2x\dx$:
\[
 \int \frac{x}{1+x^{2}}\dx = \frac{1}{2}\ln(1+x^{2}) + C.
\]
Therefore,
\[
 \int \arctan x\dx = x\arctan x - \frac{1}{2}\ln(1+x^{2}) + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int \arcsin x\dx$
content.solution:
Let $u = \arcsin x$ and $dv = dx$, so $du = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\dx$ 
and $v = x$. Then
$$
 \int \arcsin x\dx
 &= x\arcsin x - \int \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\dx.
$$
For the remaining integral, let $w = 1 - x^{2}$, $dw = -2x\dx$:
\[
 \int \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\dx = -\frac{1}{2}\int w^{-1/2}\,dw
 = -\sqrt{w} = -\sqrt{1-x^{2}} + C.
\]
Therefore,
\[
 \int \arcsin x\dx = x\arcsin x + \sqrt{1-x^{2}} + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int x\,2^{x}\dx$
content.solution:
Let $u = x$ and $dv = 2^{x}\dx$. Then $du = dx$. For $v$, recall that
$\int a^{x}\dx = \frac{a^{x}}{\ln a} + C$ when $a > 0$, $a \neq 1$. So
$v = \frac{2^{x}}{\ln 2}$.

Applying integration by parts:
$$
 \int x\,2^{x}\dx
 &= \frac{x\cdot 2^{x}}{\ln 2} - \int \frac{2^{x}}{\ln 2}\dx \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{x\cdot 2^{x}}{\ln 2} - \frac{2^{x}}{(\ln 2)^{2}} + C \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{2^{x}}{\ln 2}\left(x - \frac{1}{\ln 2}\right) + C.
$$

content.text:
Some integrals require integration by parts more than once. The principle is
the same: each application should produce a simpler integral.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int x^{2}\,e^{x}\dx$
content.solution:
**First application.** Let $u = x^{2}$, $dv = e^{x}\dx$,
so $du = 2x\dx$, $v = e^{x}$.
\[
 \int x^{2}\,e^{x}\dx
 = x^{2}\,e^{x} - 2\int x\,e^{x}\dx.
\]
The integral $\int x\,e^{x}\dx$ is the one we evaluated in
Example [ex:ibp-xex].

**Second application.** From Example [ex:ibp-xex],
$\int x\,e^{x}\dx = e^{x}(x-1) + C$. Therefore
$$
 \int x^{2}\,e^{x}\dx
 &= x^{2}\,e^{x} - 2\bigl[e^{x}(x-1)\bigr] + C \\
 &= x^{2}\,e^{x} - 2x\,e^{x} + 2\,e^{x} + C \\
 &= e^{x}(x^{2} - 2x + 2) + C.
$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int x^{2}\cos x\dx$
content.solution:
**First application.** Let $u = x^{2}$, $dv = \cos x\dx$,
so $du = 2x\dx$, $v = \sin x$.
\[
 \int x^{2}\cos x\dx = x^{2}\sin x - 2\int x\sin x\dx.
\]

**Second application.** For $\int x\sin x\dx$, let $u = x$,
$dv = \sin x\dx$, so $du = dx$, $v = -\cos x$.
\[
 \int x\sin x\dx = -x\cos x + \int \cos x\dx = -x\cos x + \sin x + C.
\]

**Combining:**
$$
 \int x^{2}\cos x\dx
 &= x^{2}\sin x - 2(-x\cos x + \sin x) + C \\
 &= x^{2}\sin x + 2x\cos x - 2\sin x + C \\
 &= (x^{2} - 2)\sin x + 2x\cos x + C.
$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\int x^{2}\sin(2x)\dx$
content.solution:
**First application.** Let $u = x^{2}$, $dv = \sin(2x)\dx$,
so $du = 2x\dx$, $v = -\frac{1}{2}\cos(2x)$.
\[
 \int x^{2}\sin(2x)\dx = -\frac{x^{2}}{2}\cos(2x) + \int x\cos(2x)\dx.
\]

**Second application.** For $\int x\cos(2x)\dx$, let $u = x$,
$dv = \cos(2x)\dx$, so $du = dx$, $v = \frac{1}{2}\sin(2x)$.
\[
 \int x\cos(2x)\dx = \frac{x}{2}\sin(2x) - \frac{1}{2}\int \sin(2x)\dx
 = \frac{x}{2}\sin(2x) + \frac{1}{4}\cos(2x) + C.
\]

**Combining:**
$$
 \int x^{2}\sin(2x)\dx
 &= -\frac{x^{2}}{2}\cos(2x) + \frac{x}{2}\sin(2x) + \frac{1}{4}\cos(2x) + C \\
 &= \frac{1}{4}(1 - 2x^{2})\cos(2x) + \frac{x}{2}\sin(2x) + C.
$$

content.text:
When one factor is a polynomial and the other is easily integrated
repeatedly (such as $e^{x}$, $\sin x$, $\cos x$), **tabular
integration** provides a compact shortcut.

content.title: Tabular Integration
content.content:
To evaluate $\int p(x)\,f(x)\dx$ where $p(x)$ is a polynomial:

1. List successive derivatives of $p(x)$ in a column until you reach $0$.

2. List successive antiderivatives of $f(x)$ in a parallel column.

3. Draw diagonal arrows from each derivative to the antiderivative one
 row below, alternating signs: $+$, $-$, $+$, $-$, …

4. The answer is the sum of the diagonal products.

content.title: Tabular method for $\displaystyle\int x^{3}\,e^{2x}\dx$
content.problem: Evaluate $\displaystyle\int x^{3}\,e^{2x}\dx$ using the tabular method.

content.title: Tabular method for $\displaystyle\int x^{4}\cos x\dx$
content.problem: Evaluate $\displaystyle\int x^{4}\cos x\dx$ using the tabular method.
content.solution:
| Sign | Derivatives of $x^{4}$ | Antiderivatives of $\cos x$ |
| $+$ | $x^{4}$ | $\sin x$ |
| $-$ | $4x^{3}$ | $-\cos x$ |
| $+$ | $12x^{2}$ | $-\sin x$ |
| $-$ | $24x$ | $\cos x$ |
| $+$ | $24$ | $\sin x$ |
| $0$ |

Reading off the products:
$$
 \int x^{4}\cos x\dx
 &= x^{4}\sin x - 4x^{3}(-\cos x) + 12x^{2}(-\sin x) \\
 &\quad {} - 24x(\cos x) + 24(\sin x) + C \\[4pt]
 &= x^{4}\sin x + 4x^{3}\cos x - 12x^{2}\sin x - 24x\cos x + 24\sin x + C \\[4pt]
 &= (x^{4} - 12x^{2} + 24)\sin x + (4x^{3} - 24x)\cos x + C.
$$

content.text:
Some integrals do not simplify after one or two applications of integration
by parts—they *return to the original integral*. Far from a dead end,
this is actually a gift: it gives us an equation we can solve.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int e^{x}\sin x\dx$
content.solution:
Let $I = \int e^{x}\sin x\dx$.

**First application.** Let $u = \sin x$, $dv = e^{x}\dx$,
so $du = \cos x\dx$, $v = e^{x}$.
\[
 I = e^{x}\sin x - \int e^{x}\cos x\dx.
\]

**Second application.** For the remaining integral, let $u = \cos x$,
$dv = e^{x}\dx$, so $du = -\sin x\dx$, $v = e^{x}$.
\[
 \int e^{x}\cos x\dx
 = e^{x}\cos x - \int e^{x}(-\sin x)\dx
 = e^{x}\cos x + \int e^{x}\sin x\dx
 = e^{x}\cos x + I.
\]
Substituting back:
\[
 I = e^{x}\sin x - \bigl(e^{x}\cos x + I\bigr)
 = e^{x}\sin x - e^{x}\cos x - I.
\]
Now solve for $I$:
\[
 2I = e^{x}(\sin x - \cos x),
 \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
 I = \frac{e^{x}(\sin x - \cos x)}{2} + C.
\]

content.title: Consistency in the circular method
content.content:
When applying integration by parts twice in the circular method, you must
make the *same type of choice* each time. If you let $u$ be the
trigonometric factor in the first step, you must also let $u$ be the
trigonometric factor in the second step. Switching choices will simply undo
your work and yield $0 = 0$.

content.title: $\displaystyle\int e^{x}\cos x\dx$
content.solution:
Let $J = \int e^{x}\cos x\dx$.

**First application.** Let $u = \cos x$, $dv = e^{x}\dx$,
so $du = -\sin x\dx$, $v = e^{x}$.
\[
 J = e^{x}\cos x + \int e^{x}\sin x\dx.
\]

**Second application.** For $\int e^{x}\sin x\dx$, let $u = \sin x$,
$dv = e^{x}\dx$:
\[
 \int e^{x}\sin x\dx = e^{x}\sin x - \int e^{x}\cos x\dx = e^{x}\sin x - J.
\]

Substituting back:
\[
 J = e^{x}\cos x + e^{x}\sin x - J.
\]
Solving for $J$:
\[
 2J = e^{x}(\cos x + \sin x),
 \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
 J = \frac{e^{x}(\cos x + \sin x)}{2} + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int e^{2x}\sin(3x)\dx$
content.solution:
Let $I = \int e^{2x}\sin(3x)\dx$.

**First application.** Let $u = \sin(3x)$, $dv = e^{2x}\dx$,
so $du = 3\cos(3x)\dx$, $v = \frac{1}{2}e^{2x}$.
\[
 I = \frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\sin(3x) - \frac{3}{2}\int e^{2x}\cos(3x)\dx.
\]

**Second application.** For $\int e^{2x}\cos(3x)\dx$, let
$u = \cos(3x)$, $dv = e^{2x}\dx$:
\[
 \int e^{2x}\cos(3x)\dx = \frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\cos(3x)
 + \frac{3}{2}\int e^{2x}\sin(3x)\dx
 = \frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\cos(3x) + \frac{3}{2}I.
\]

Substituting back:
\[
 I = \frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\sin(3x)
 - \frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\cos(3x) + \frac{3}{2}I\right)
 = \frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\sin(3x) - \frac{3}{4}e^{2x}\cos(3x) - \frac{9}{4}I.
\]

Solving:
\[
 I + \frac{9}{4}I = \frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\sin(3x) - \frac{3}{4}e^{2x}\cos(3x),
 \qquad
 \frac{13}{4}I = \frac{e^{2x}}{4}\bigl(2\sin(3x) - 3\cos(3x)\bigr).
\]
\[
 I = \frac{e^{2x}(2\sin(3x) - 3\cos(3x))}{13} + C.
\]

content.title: $\displaystyle\int e^{-x}\cos(2x)\dx$
content.solution:
Let $I = \int e^{-x}\cos(2x)\dx$.

**First application.** Let $u = \cos(2x)$, $dv = e^{-x}\dx$:
\[
 I = -e^{-x}\cos(2x) - 2\int e^{-x}\sin(2x)\dx.
\]

**Second application.** For $\int e^{-x}\sin(2x)\dx$:
\[
 \int e^{-x}\sin(2x)\dx = -e^{-x}\sin(2x) + 2\int e^{-x}\cos(2x)\dx
 = -e^{-x}\sin(2x) + 2I.
\]

Substituting back:
\[
 I = -e^{-x}\cos(2x) - 2(-e^{-x}\sin(2x) + 2I)
 = -e^{-x}\cos(2x) + 2e^{-x}\sin(2x) - 4I.
\]

Solving:
\[
 5I = e^{-x}(2\sin(2x) - \cos(2x)),
 \qquad
 I = \frac{e^{-x}(2\sin(2x) - \cos(2x))}{5} + C.
\]

content.text:
Integration by parts can produce **reduction formulas**—recursive
identities that express an integral in terms of a simpler integral of the
same type.

content.title: Reduction formula for $\displaystyle\int \sin^{n}x\dx$
content.problem:
Derive a reduction formula for $\displaystyle\int \sin^{n}x\dx$, where
$n \geq 2$.
content.solution:
Write $\sin^{n}x = \sin^{n-1}x \cdot \sin x$ and let
\[
 u = \sin^{n-1}x, \qquad dv = \sin x\dx,
\]
so $du = (n-1)\sin^{n-2}x\,\cos x\dx$ and $v = -\cos x$. Then
$$
 \int \sin^{n}x\dx
 &= -\sin^{n-1}x\,\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\,\cos^{2}x\dx.
$$
Replace $\cos^{2}x = 1 - \sin^{2}x$:
$$
 \int \sin^{n}x\dx
 &= -\sin^{n-1}x\,\cos x
 + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\dx
 - (n-1)\int \sin^{n}x\dx.
$$
Move the last term to the left side:
\[
 n\int \sin^{n}x\dx
 = -\sin^{n-1}x\,\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\dx.
\]
Dividing by $n$:
\[
 \int \sin^{n}x\dx
 = -\frac{\sin^{n-1}x\,\cos x}{n}
 + \frac{n-1}{n}\int \sin^{n-2}x\dx.
\]
Each application reduces the exponent by $2$, eventually reaching
$\int \sin x\dx$ or $\int 1\dx$.

content.title: Using the reduction formula
content.solution:
Using the reduction formula with $n = 4$:
\[
 \int \sin^{4}x\dx = -\frac{\sin^{3}x\cos x}{4}
 + \frac{3}{4}\int \sin^{2}x\dx.
\]
Now apply it again with $n = 2$:
\[
 \int \sin^{2}x\dx = -\frac{\sin x\cos x}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\int 1\dx
 = -\frac{\sin x\cos x}{2} + \frac{x}{2} + C.
\]
Therefore:
$$
 \int \sin^{4}x\dx
 &= -\frac{\sin^{3}x\cos x}{4}
 + \frac{3}{4}\left(-\frac{\sin x\cos x}{2} + \frac{x}{2}\right) + C \\
 &= -\frac{\sin^{3}x\cos x}{4} - \frac{3\sin x\cos x}{8} + \frac{3x}{8} + C.
$$
Using $\sin x\cos x = \frac{1}{2}\sin(2x)$, this can be written as
\[
 \int \sin^{4}x\dx = \frac{3x}{8} - \frac{\sin(2x)}{4}
 + \frac{\sin(4x)}{32} + C.
\]

content.title: Reduction formula for $\displaystyle\int (\ln x)^{n}\dx$
content.problem: Derive a reduction formula for $\displaystyle\int (\ln x)^{n}\dx$.
content.solution:
Let $u = (\ln x)^{n}$ and $dv = dx$, so $du = \frac{n(\ln x)^{n-1}}{x}\dx$
and $v = x$.
$$
 \int (\ln x)^{n}\dx
 &= x(\ln x)^{n} - \int x \cdot \frac{n(\ln x)^{n-1}}{x}\dx \\
 &= x(\ln x)^{n} - n\int (\ln x)^{n-1}\dx.
$$
This gives the reduction formula:
\[
 \int (\ln x)^{n}\dx = x(\ln x)^{n} - n\int (\ln x)^{n-1}\dx.
\]

content.title: Using the logarithm reduction formula
content.solution:
Applying the reduction formula repeatedly:
$$
 \int (\ln x)^{3}\dx &= x(\ln x)^{3} - 3\int (\ln x)^{2}\dx \\
 &= x(\ln x)^{3} - 3\left[x(\ln x)^{2} - 2\int \ln x\dx\right] \\
 &= x(\ln x)^{3} - 3x(\ln x)^{2} + 6\int \ln x\dx \\
 &= x(\ln x)^{3} - 3x(\ln x)^{2} + 6(x\ln x - x) + C \\
 &= x(\ln x)^{3} - 3x(\ln x)^{2} + 6x\ln x - 6x + C \\
 &= x\bigl[(\ln x)^{3} - 3(\ln x)^{2} + 6\ln x - 6\bigr] + C.
$$

content.title: Reduction formula for $\displaystyle\int x^{n}e^{x}\dx$
content.problem: Derive and use a reduction formula for $\displaystyle\int x^{n}e^{x}\dx$.

content.text: Definite Integrals Using Integration by Parts

content.title: Definite integral using IBP
content.solution:
By LIATE, let $u = \ln x$ and $dv = x^{2}\dx$, so $du = \dfrac{1}{x}\dx$
and $v = \dfrac{x^{3}}{3}$. Then
$$
 \defint{1}{e} x^{2}\ln x\dx
 &= \left[\frac{x^{3}}{3}\ln x\right]_{1}^{e}
 - \defint{1}{e} \frac{x^{3}}{3}\cdot\frac{1}{x}\dx \\[4pt]
 &= \left[\frac{x^{3}}{3}\ln x\right]_{1}^{e}
 - \frac{1}{3}\defint{1}{e} x^{2}\dx \\[4pt]
 &= \left(\frac{e^{3}}{3}\cdot 1 - \frac{1}{3}\cdot 0\right)
 - \frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{1}^{e} \\[4pt]
 &= \frac{e^{3}}{3}
 - \frac{1}{9}\bigl(e^{3} - 1\bigr)
 = \frac{3e^{3} - e^{3} + 1}{9}
 = \frac{2e^{3} + 1}{9}.
$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\defint{0}{\pi}x\sin x\dx$
content.solution:
Let $u = x$, $dv = \sin x\dx$, so $du = dx$, $v = -\cos x$.
$$
 \defint{0}{\pi}x\sin x\dx
 &= \bigl[-x\cos x\bigr]_{0}^{\pi} + \defint{0}{\pi}\cos x\dx \\
 &= \bigl[-\pi\cos\pi - 0\bigr] + \bigl[\sin x\bigr]_{0}^{\pi} \\
 &= -\pi(-1) + (0 - 0) = \pi.
$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\defint{0}^{\pi/2}x^{2}\cos x\dx$
content.solution:
Using the result from Example [ex:ibp-x2cosx]:
\[
 \int x^{2}\cos x\dx = (x^{2}-2)\sin x + 2x\cos x + C.
\]
Therefore:
$$
 \defint{0}^{\pi/2}x^{2}\cos x\dx
 &= \bigl[(x^{2}-2)\sin x + 2x\cos x\bigr]_{0}^{\pi/2} \\
 &= \left[\left(\frac{\pi^{2}}{4} - 2\right)(1) + \pi(0)\right]
 - \bigl[(-2)(0) + 0\bigr] \\
 &= \frac{\pi^{2}}{4} - 2.
$$

content.title: Brook Taylor (1685--1731)
content.content:
Brook Taylor, remembered today for Taylor series, also contributed to the
development of integration by parts. His 1715 work *Methodus
Incrementorum Directa et Inversa* systematized many of the finite-difference
and summation-by-parts techniques that preceded the integral version. Taylor
was a devout Anglican who saw mathematics as a window into divine order. He
wrote: "An equation of a curve is its innermost essence made manifest."

content.title: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646--1716)
content.content:
Leibniz, co-inventor of calculus, developed the notation $\int u\,dv$ that
makes integration by parts so elegant. His notation was designed to be
"suggestive"—to guide the mind toward correct manipulation. Leibniz was
a polymath who made contributions to philosophy, logic, physics, and
diplomacy. He believed in a pre-established harmony between mind and nature,
ordained by God, that made mathematics possible.

marginNotes.text:
It is the glory of God to conceal a matter;
to search out a matter is the glory of kings.

Whatever you do, work heartily, as for the
Lord and not for men.

Do not be conformed to this world, but be
transformed by the renewal of your mind.

For everything there is a season, and a
time for every matter under heaven.

And let us not grow weary of doing good, for
in due season we will reap, if we do not give up.

Great are the works of the Lord, studied by all
who delight in them.

Do you not know that in a race all the
runners run, but only one receives the prize? So run that you may obtain it.

I press on toward the goal for the prize
of the upward call of God in Christ Jesus.

marginNotes.author: George P\'olya
marginNotes.text:
If you can't solve a problem, then there is an
easier problem you can solve: find it.

marginNotes.author: Isaac Newton
marginNotes.text:
If I have seen further, it is by standing on the
shoulders of giants.

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal
marginNotes.text:
I have made this letter longer than usual because
I lack the time to make it shorter.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler
marginNotes.text:
Nothing takes place in the world whose meaning
is not that of some maximum or minimum.
